coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen functionlized graphene quantum dots/3D bismuth oxyiodine hybrid hollow microspheres as remarkable photoelectrode for photoelectrochemical sensing of chlopyrifos
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qian
Yin, Yuanyuan
Hao, Nan
Qian, Jing
Li, Libo
You, Tianyan
Mao, Hanping
Wang, Kun
coredata.dc:description: This work presents a selective and reliable visible-light driven photoelectrochemical (PEC) chlopyrifos sensor using nitrogen functionalized graphene quantum dots/3D bismuth oxyiodine (NFGQDs/3DBiOI) hybrid hollow microspheres (HHMs) as a remarkable photoelectrode. In this protocol, a facile one-pot solvothermal reaction was used for the synthesis of the new NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs, which achieved significant enhancement (ca. 18-fold) photocurrent generation compared to pure BiOI microspheres by virtue of the band gap narrowing and the electron transfer acceleration of such HHMs. In addition, the introduction of chlorpyrifos pesticide enabled the formation of Bi-chlorpyrifos complex which resulted in quenching the charge transfer efficiency, thus leading to a decrease in photocurrent intensity and selective PEC recording of chlorpyrifos level. This novel PEC sensor exerted predominant performance for chlorpyrifos detection including wide linear range (0.1–50 ng mL−1), low detection limit (0.03 ng mL−1), satisfactory selectivity and also good feasibility in real water sample analysis. The strategy presented here implying that a three-dimensional hollow nanostructure would play a crucial role in developing multifarious novel detection methodologies in PEC sensing fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen functionalized graphene quantum dot
Bismuth oxyiodine
Hybrid hollow microsphere
Photoelectrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NFGQDs and NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs
Enhanced photocurrent generation of NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs
PEC detection of chlorpyrifos pesticide based on NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs
Possible mechanism for PEC detection of chlorpyrifos
Selectivity, stability, reproducibility and real sample analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was financial supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375050, 21675066, 31671584 and 61601204), Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu (No. BK20160542), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Nos. 2015M581745 and 2017T100330), Jiangsu Province Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 1601204C) and Research Foundation of Zhenjiang Science and Technology Bureau (No. NY2016011).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Illustration for the synthesis process of NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs; (B) the XRD patterns of the as-prepared BiOI (a) and NFGQDs/3DBiOI samples (b); (C) SEM image and (D) high-magnification SEM image of the NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs; (E) SEM image of pure BiOI microspheres, inset: the photographs of the as-prepared BiOI and NFGQDs/3DBiOI samples.
XPS spectra of the NFGQDs/3DBiOI sample: (A) survey; (B) Bi 4f; (C) I 3d; (D) C 1s and (E) O 1s.

(A) Photocurrent responses of BiOI and NFGQDs/3DBiOI/ITO for ten on-off cycles of intermittent irradiation in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0); (B) EIS responses of BiOI (a), NFGQDs/3DBiOI (b) and NFGQDs (c) modified ITO electrode in 0.1 M KCl with 5.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−; (C) plots of (Ahυ)2 versus the energy (hυ) for the band gap energy of BiOI (a) and NFGQDs/3DBiOI samples (b).

(A) Photocurrent responses of the NFGQDs/3DBiOI/ITO in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 1 ng mL−1 chlorpyrifos; (B) photocurrent responses of the NFGQDs/3DBiOI/ITO in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) in the presence of 0 (a), 0.1 (b), 0.5 (c), 1.0 (d), 5.0 (e), 10.0 (f) and 50.0 (g) ng mL−1 chlorpyrifos; (C) the calibration curve for chlorpyrifos detection.

(A) The illustration of the detection mechanism of the PEC chlorpyrifos sensor; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of 1.0 mg mL−1 BiOI (a), 1.0 mg mL−1 BiOI containing 0.01 mg mL−1 (b) and 0.10 mg mL−1 (c) of chlorpyrifos, and 0.10 mg mL−1 free chlorpyrifos solution (d); (C) EIS of NFGQDs/3DBiOI/ITO before (a) and after (b) the addition of 0.10 mg mL−1 of chlorpyrifos.
Effects of different pesticides on the photocurrent intensity of the NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs modified ITO electrode.
Comparison of analytical performance for chlorpyrifos detection in reported methods.
Determination of chlorpyrifos in water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen functionlized graphene quantum dots/3D bismuth oxyiodine hybrid hollow microspheres as remarkable photoelectrode for photoelectrochemical sensing of chlopyrifos

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents a selective and reliable visible-light driven photoelectrochemical (PEC) chlopyrifos sensor using nitrogen functionalized graphene quantum dots/3D bismuth oxyiodine (NFGQDs/3DBiOI) hybrid hollow microspheres (HHMs) as a remarkable photoelectrode. In this protocol, a facile one-pot solvothermal reaction was used for the synthesis of the new NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs, which achieved significant enhancement (ca. 18-fold) photocurrent generation compared to pure BiOI microspheres by virtue of the band gap narrowing and the electron transfer acceleration of such HHMs. In addition, the introduction of chlorpyrifos pesticide enabled the formation of Bi-chlorpyrifos complex which resulted in quenching the charge transfer efficiency, thus leading to a decrease in photocurrent intensity and selective PEC recording of chlorpyrifos level. This novel PEC sensor exerted predominant performance for chlorpyrifos detection including wide linear range (0.1–50 ng mL−1), low detection limit (0.03 ng mL−1), satisfactory selectivity and also good feasibility in real water sample analysis. The strategy presented here implying that a three-dimensional hollow nanostructure would play a crucial role in developing multifarious novel detection methodologies in PEC sensing fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing, a recently emerged methodology that integrating of PEC process with electrochemical analysis, is becoming a subject of new research interests due to its attractive potential for various sensing applications [1,2]. In a typical PEC process, light is used as excitation source and photocurrent is the readout signal, because of this total separation and the different energy form of the excitation source and detection signal, PEC sensing normally exhibits lower background signal and higher sensitivity compared with the conventional electrochemical ones [1,3]. Generally, PEC sensor necessitates the use of a PEC active material-based photoelectrode to convert photoirradiation to electrical signal, and the high photo-electric conversion efficiency of photoelectrode was beneficial for increasing the sensitivity of a PEC sensor [4–6]. Thus, it is of great importance to pursue efficient PEC active photoelectrode with outstanding photo-electric conversion efficiency.

Bismuth oxyiodine (BiOI), an important V-VI-VII ternary semiconductor compound, exhibits relatively superior visible-light-induced photoactivity, which affords great opportunities in diverse environmental and energy-related applications [7–9]. In fact, the pure BiOI material was far from satisfaction due to the low photo-to-current conversion efficiencies [10]. Materials with hollow structures, which could effectively harvest light through multiple scattering of light, were proven to be an ideal candidate in a large spectrum of PEC applications especially in the engineering of novel PEC sensing interfaces [11–13]. In the past few years, investigations on the controllable synthesis of BiOI microspheres with special hollow structure have been reported [14–16]. It is worth mentioning that extra additives such as ionic liquid and polyvinyl pyrrolidone polymer were necessary in the formation of such BiOI hollow microspheres, which made the reaction system complicated and costly. Considering the complicated and expensive methods for the synthesis of hollow microsphere materials, it is highly desirable to develop a facile and economic approach for the preparation of BiOI-based hollow structures with improved performance.

Given a specific semiconductor material, surface modifications are important not only to activate the semiconductor for PEC applications but also to facilitate charge separation. Recently, some strategies have been successfully employed to improve the photoactivity of BiOI by hybridizing them with other nanomaterials (semiconductor, metal, and carbon-based materials) to facilitate photo-induced electron-hole separation [17–22]. For example, Chang et al. have hybridized BiOI with mesoporous graphite-like carbon nitride to form heterojunction structure, and the photocatalytic performance of the BiOI-based heterojunction was improved and much higher than pure BiOI for the degradation of bisphenol A under visible-light irradiation [20]. Except for the potential applications of BiOI-based composites in photocatalysis, recently, Yan et al. empolyed the BiOI/graphene as an effective photoactive material for the fabrication of photoelectrohemical (PEC) sensors, and the photocurrent response of photocathode was promoted by doping BiOI with a suitable amount of graphene [22]. These investigations above showed a promising approach in the fabrication of BiOI related functionalized composites with preferable performance in the practical applications of environmental pollutants detection and removal. Nitrogen functionalized graphene quantum dots (NFGQDs), a significant carbon nanomaterial doped with nitrogen atoms, have ignited tremendous interests as a novel enhanced material for improving the photoelectrical performance of semiconductor materials due to their unique electrical conductivity [23,24]. In this work, the NFGQDs was employed as the template and also the dopant for the synthesis of NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs, and therefore, it may have the advantage of a simple synthetic process and better chemical properties.

Herein, we report an efficient PEC sensing platform for selective detection of chlorpyrifos using NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs as photoelectrode. The NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs with NFGQDs supported on BiOI nanostructure were synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. The superior photoactivity of NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs was verified and used for the fabrication of effective sensing platform. Based on the complexation effect between the PEC active NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs and chlorpyrifos, a PEC sensor for chlorpyrifos detection was finally developed.

Glycol, potassium iodide (KI), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Chlorpyrifos, parathion-methyl, acetamiprid, and pentachlorophenol were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. 0.1 M phosphate buffered solution (PBS, pH 7.0) was used as the supporting electrolyte, which was prepared by mixing stock standard solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout the study.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were conducted on a Bruker D8 diffractometer with high-intensity Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 Å). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were performed on a field-emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL JSM-7001F). UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were measured by UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a VG MultiLab 2000 system with a monochromatic Mg-Kα source operated at 20 kV. The PEC and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted with a conventional three-electrode system where a modified ITO electrode, platinum wire and saturated calomel electrode was employed as working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode. All the PEC measurements were performed using a CHI660 B electrochemical workstation (Chen Hua Instruments, Shanghai, China) with a 250 W Xe lamp (Beijing Trusttech Co. Ltd.) as the visible-light source. The EIS measurements was carried out in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NFGQDs and NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs
ce:section.ce:para: The NFGQDs were synthesized by an easy reflux approach according to our previous work [25]. In brief, 15 mL ammonium citrate aqueous solution (33 mg mL−1) was put into a three-necked flask and the reaction temperature was maintained at 200 °C under reflux for 0.5 h. The color of the ammonium citrate aqueous solution was changed from colorless to orange in the reaction process, indicating the formation of NFGQDs. Then the pH of the obtained NFGQDs solution was adjusted to 7.0 with NaOH solution (10 mg mL−1) and ultimately, the resulting NFGQDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs were prepared via a facile one-pot solvothermal method using NFGQDs and Bi(NO3)3·5H2O as precursor (shown in Fig. 1 A). Typically, 10 mL of 0.015 mol L−1 Bi(NO3)3·5H2O dissolved in glycol was added drop-wisely into the same volume of 0.015 mol L−1 KI/glycol solution, then, a certain amount (1.3 mL) of NFGQDs was added in the above mixed solution. The mixture was vigorously stirred at room temperature for 60 min and transferred to a 25 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave, which was sealed in a stainless steel tank and maintained at 140 °C for 12 h. Finally, the resulting precipitates were collected and washed with ethanol and deionized for thrice, followed by the drying in oven at 50 °C to obtain NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs. For comparison, pure BiOI samples were prepared by the similar method in the absence of NFGQDs.

Prior to the modification, the ITO electrodes were placed into the boiling water which containing 0.1 M NaOH for 10 min, and then were ultrasonically cleaned with water and alcohol for 5 min. For the ITO electrode modification, 2 mg of NFGQDs/3DBiOI sample was dispersed ultrasonically in 1 mL DMF to obtain a 2 mg mL−1 suspension. Then, 35 μL of the resulting dispersion was drop-cast onto a piece of ITO slice and dried in air at room temperature to obtain NFGQDs/3DBiOI modified ITO electrode (denoted as NFGQDs/3DBiOI/ITO). For comparison, the BiOI/ITO was prepared by the same method with 2 mg mL−1 BiOI suspension.

The phase and purity of the products were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. As shown in Fig. 1B, several major diffraction peaks were observed for the pure BiOI (curve a) and NFGQDs/BiOI microspheres (curve b), which were readily indexed to the tetragonal phase of BiOI (JCPDS card no. 10-0445) with well-resolved (101), (102), (110), (104), (212) and (220) reflections [26]. Compared with pure BiOI, no peaks from NFGQDs were detected from the XRD pattern of NFGQDs/3DBiOI, which was due to a little amount of NFGQDs that cannot be resolved by XRD [27]. In addition, no other diffraction peaks was observed for both the pure BiOI and NFGQDs/3DBiOI samples, indicating the high-purity of obtained samples without formation of impurities [28]. The panoramic morphologies of the as-synthesized NFGQDs/3DBiOI hollow microspheres were examined by SEM. Fig. 1C presented a typical low-magnification SEM image of the sample, it was obvious that the product was composed of large-scale microspheres, and most of the microspheres were attached to each other to form large microsphere aggregates. In addition, some of the microspheres had a hollow interior in the center with diameters of about 100 nm which were easily to be seen. From the high-magnification SEM observation in Fig. 3C inset, it could be easily found that each microsphere was consisted of numerous nanosheets which were somewhat out-of-flatness and arranged rulessly to the centre of the sphere. By comparison, the pure BiOI microsphere (prepared without the addition of NFGQDs) presented the flower-like structures, which were consisted of numerous nanosheets with a mean diameter of 2 μm (as shown in Fig. 1E).

To further understand the evaluation of the purity and surface composition of the samples, XPS spectrum was collected for NFGQDs/3DBiOI materials. The survey XPS spectrum in Fig. 2
A confirmed the existence of Bi, O, I, and C elements on the surface of NFGQDs/3DBiOI. It could be observed at binding energies of 158.5 and 163.8 eV originated from Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 in Fig. 2B, which were corresponding to Bi3+ in the BiOI microspheres [29]. Fig. 2C showed two strong characteristic peaks of I− in BiOI at 619.2 eV (I 3d5/2) and 630.5 eV (I 3d3/2) [17]. Fig. 2D revealed the expanded XPS spectrum of the C 1s peaks at 284.4, 285.1, and 288.2 eV, respectively, which could be assigned to CC, CN, and CO in NFGQDs [30]. Furthermore, the high resolution of O 1s XPS spectrum could be associated with three peaks at binding energies of 529.5, 531.0, and 532.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 2E). The peak at 529.5 eV and 531.0 eV were attributed to the BiO bonds in [Bi2O2] slabs of BiOI and IO bonds in BiOI, while the peak at 532.5 eV indicated the presence of other components such as OH, O2, and H2O adsorbed on the surface [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced photocurrent generation of NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs
ce:section.ce:para: The photocurrent signals of pure BiOI and NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs were investigated upon visible-light activited photoexcitation. As illustrated in Fig. 3A, a small PEC response of pure BiOI microspheres (∼0.12 μA) was observed (curve a); while the photocurrent of NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs modified electrode was nearly 18-fold higher than that of pure BiOI (curve b), which exhibited substantial improved photocurrent generation of NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs than pure BiOI microspheres by virtue of the NFGQDs incorporation. On the one hand, the addition of NFGQDs could modulate the morphology of BiOI spheres with such special hollow structure; on the other hand, the NFGQDs as dopant could hybrid with BiOI for the formation of hybrid spheres. Both the hollow structure of the materials and also the surface modifications could improve the photo-to-current conversion efficiencies of the photoactive materials, inducing the enhanced photocurrent of the NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs [12,18]. Moreover, stable photocurrents were observed at the NFGQDs/3DBiOI sample for ten on–off cycles of intermittent irradiation (Fig. 3A), indicating the good stability of the photocurrent signal.

To better understand the underlying mechanism of the enhanced PEC generation of NFGQDs/3DBiOI sample, the EIS of the pure BiOI and NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs were investigated. And the equivalent circuit (inset of Fig. 3B) was used for fitting the impedance data, where Rs , Rct , Zw , and Q represent electrolyte solution resistance, charge-transfer resistance, Warburg impedance, and constant phase elements, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 3B, the BiOI modified ITO had an Rct of 6560 Ω (curve a), after NFGQDs doping, the Rct of NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs was decreased dramatically (curve b). The NFGQDs exhibited the smallest Rct value of 820 Ω (curve i) due to their inherent fast electron transfer rate [23]. In addition, the EIS results above were accordance with the PEC response of the samples in Fig. 3A, illustrating the fact that the enhanced photocurrent of NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs were attributed to the fast electron transfer rate by the incorporation of NFGQDs and the unique hollow microstructures of the samples [12]. Furthermore, the band gap energy (Eg ) of the pure BiOI and NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs was also studied. According to the previous report, the Eg of semiconductor material could be calculated from the following equation: αE photon = K(E photon-E g)n/2, where α was absorption coefficient, E photon was the discrete photoenergy, K was a constant for the material, and n was 2 for a direct transition or 4 for an indirect transition [33]. Fig. 3C showed the plots for the relationship of (αE photon)1/2 versus E photon, indicating that the bandgap of NFGQDs/3DBiOI samples was calculated to be 1.93 eV, which was narrower than that of pure BiOI (2.14 eV) and beneficial for the amplification of the visible-light driven PEC activity of the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC detection of chlorpyrifos pesticide based on NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs
ce:section.ce:para: The enhanced photocurrent generation of the NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs with good stability has an advantage for a better performance in the PEC sensing system. In this work, the PEC sensing performance of the NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs was tested by the detection of chlorpyrifos pesticide, which has a potential health risk to human beings and animals. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the addition of chlorpyrifos resulted in a significant decrease in the photocurrent intensity, indicating the resulting PEC system based on NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs could be used for chlorpyrifos monitoring effectively. Fig. 4B showed the photocurrent intensity of NFGQDs/3DBiOI modified electrode corresponded to the concentration of chlorpyrifos from 0.1 to 50 ng mL−1, it is obvious that the photocurrents decreased gradually with the concentrations of chlorpyrifos increased. By analyzing the photocurrent with the concentrations of chlorpyrifos, the photocurrents displayed a good linear relationship between the photocurrent intensity (I) and the logarithm of chlorpyrifos concentration (Fig. 4C). The linear equation were I = −0.305 lgC + 1.876 (R 2 = 0.998) over a concentration range from 0.1 to 50 ng mL−1. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.03 ng mL−1 at the signal noise ratio of 3. In addition, the performance of the proposed chlorpyrifos sensor was compared with other reported methods in literatures [34–38], which were summarized in Table 1 . As can be seen, the proposed PEC sensor showed good sensing properties with a relative wide linear range and a low detection limit, which indicated that this method exhibited promise in sensitive chlorpyrifos determination. Such good performance of the NFGQDs/3DBiOI based PEC sensor for chlorpyrifos detection was attributed to the higher sensitivity of the PEC method and the super PEC activity of the photoactive NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for PEC detection of chlorpyrifos
ce:section.ce:para: The possible mechanism for the PEC sensing of chlopyrifos based on NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs was described in Fig. 5 A, which could be summarized as follows: the addition of chlorpyrifos could be combined with the Bi3+ on the NFGQDs/3DBiOI microspheres to form Bi-chlorpyrifos complex, inducing a rise in steric hindrance which blocked the electron transfer process from BiOI to NFGQDs, consequently resulting in a decrease in photocurrent intensity [32]. In order to further certify the mechanism stated above, the interaction between BiOI and chlorpyrifos was confirmed by the detailed UV–vis absorbance spectra, which was a simple and applicable method to investigate the formation of complex. As shown in Fig. 5B, a strong absorption of 1.0 mg mL−1 BiOI microspheres was observed at ca. 227 nm (curve a), with the addition of 0.01 mg mL−1 (curve b) and 0.1 mg mL−1 (curve c) chlorpyrifos, the absorption spectra of BiOI microspheres were both red-shifted slightly. Moreover, as the concentration of chlorpyrifos increased, a new peak at about 305 nm appeared (curve c) and also displayed a red shift compared with pure chlorpyrifos (curve d). It could be described as obvious evidence that the Bi-chlorpyrifos complex with new structure was formed [39,40]. In addition, the blocking electron transfer of the resulting Bi-chlorpyrifos complex assembled on the surface of the NFGQDs/3DBiOI was further confirmed by the EIS analysis. As shown in Fig. 5C, the increase of R ct value (from 970 Ω in curve a to 4510 Ω in curve b) was observed after the injecting of chlorpyrifos to the electrochemical system, indicating that the electro transfer rate was blocked by the addition of chlorpyrifos, which further confirmed the possibility of the chlorpyrifos detection mechanisim discussed above. According to the detection mechanism present here, the proposed PEC sensor could be used in the detection of other molecules and compounds which have the similar structure with chlorpyrifos.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability, reproducibility and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed PEC sensor was studied by selecting some representative interfering pesticides including parathion-methyl, acetamiprid, and pentachlorophenol. As shown in Fig. 6 , it was observed that the NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs were much more sensitive to chlorpyrifos than to other different pesticides. This is due to the fact that among the four pesticides, only chlorpyrifos could provide the CN and PS bonds, which could be bonded with Bi3+ to form Bi-chlorpyrifos structure (as shown in Fig. 6 inset). To explore the stability of the PEC sensor, the obtained sensing platform was stored at room temperature for 12 days, the photocurrent results indicated that no significantly change was observed for 1 ng mL−1 chlorpyrifos, indicating the satisfied long-term storage stability of the proposed PEC sensor. Moreover, the reproducibility of the proposed sensor was studied by an interassay relative standard deviation (RSD) through testing 5 ng mL−1 chlorpyrifos with three independent electrodes which were modified at the same experimental condition, and RSD of 7.6% was calculated, suggesting the acceptable reproducibility of the PEC sensor.

The feasibility of the obtained PEC sensor was assessed using standard addition method, the water samples was collected from Yangtze River in Zhenjiang city. As listed in Table 2 , 0.5, 3.0 and 10.0 ng mL−1 of standard chlorpyrifos solutions were added into the real water samples. The average recoveries were in the range of 100.0–107.0%, which is higher than the added values of chlorpyrifos. Except for this, the recovery results of the PEC sensor for river water were higher than tap water, indicating the positive deviation was likely attributed to the existing interfering substances in the river water samples. In addition, the HPLC results were compared with the proposed PEC sensor for chlorpyrifos detection in real samples in Table 2. The satisfactory results for real water sample analysis implied the reliability and potential applicability of the resulted sensor for chlorpyrifos determination in real environmental samples.

In summary, an efficient sensing platform for selective PEC monitoring of chlorpyrifos was developed using NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs, which were synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method with the assistance of NFGQDs. As an effective photoelectrode material, the NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs exhibited improved PEC activity than pure BiOI microspheres, and the mechanism of the improved PEC activity was further studied. The proposed PEC sensor showed good performance for chlorpyrifos determination including wide linear range, low detection limit, satisfactory selectivity and reliability in real sample analysis. It is anticipated that the NFGQDs/3DBiOI HHMs proposed here can be easily extended to various PEC applications for biosensors or biofuels.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was financial supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21375050, 21675066, 31671584 and 61601204), Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu (No. BK20160542), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Nos. 2015M581745 and 2017T100330), Jiangsu Province Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 1601204C) and Research Foundation of Zhenjiang Science and Technology Bureau (No. NY2016011).

A cathodic signal-off photoelectrochemical aptasensor for ultrasensitive and selective detection of oxytetracycline
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
N-doped graphene quantum dots-functionalized titanium dioxide nanofibers and their highly efficient photocurrent response
Atmospheric pressure synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots for fabrication of BiOBr nanohybrids with enhanced visible-light photoactivity and photostability
Preparation of sphere-like g-C3N4/BiOI photocatalysts via a reactable ionic liquid for visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of pollutants
Improved visible light photocatalytic activity of MWCNT/BiOBr composite synthesized via a reactable ionic liquid
Hydrothermal preparation of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)/CdS nanocomposite and its efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production under visible light irradiation
Heterogeneous photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants over titanium dioxide: a review of fundamentals, progress and problems
Nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate
Photocatalytic degradation kinetics and mechanism of antivirus drug-lamivudine in TiO2 dispersion
Synthesis and characterization of Pt/BiOI nanoplate catalyst with enhanced activity under visible light irradiation
Highly sensitive photoelectrochemical assay for DNA methyltransferase activity and inhibitor screening by exciton energy transfer coupled with enzyme cleavage biosensing strategy
Liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry method for the simultaneous quantitative determination of the organophosphorus pesticides dimethoate fenthion, diazinon and chlorpyrifos in human blood
Determination of malation methidathion, and chlorpyrifos ethyl pesticides using acetylcholinesterase biosensor based on Nafion/Ag@rGO-NH2 nanocomposites
A visible light photoelectrochemical biosensor coupling enzyme-inhibition for organophosphates monitoring based on a dual-functional Cd0.5Zn0.5S-reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite
Poly(3-hexylthiophene)/TiO2 nanoparticle-functionalized electrodes for visible light and low potential photoelectrochemical sensing of organophosphorus pesticide chlopyrifos
Visible light photoelectrochemical sensor based on Au nanoparticles and molecularly imprinted poly(o-phenylenediamine)-modified TiO2 nanotubes for specific and sensitive detection chlorpyrifos
Molecular interactions of isoxazolcurcumin with human serum albumin: spectroscopic and molecular modeling studies
Spectroscopic, structural and thermodynamic properties of chlorpyrifos bound to serum albumin: a comparative study between BSA and HAS

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qian Liu obtained her Ph.D. from Jiangsu University (China) in 2014. Now she is a postdoctor at School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her current research interests focus on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in pesticides and biomolecules analysis in agriculture.

Yuanyuan Yin is currently studying for master degree in school of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her main research interest lies in the field of photoelectrochemical sensor.

Nan Hao obtained his Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from Nanjing University (China) in 2015. Now he is a lecturer at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His current research interests focus on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in bioanalysis.

Jing Qian obtained her Ph.D. in Material Physics and Chemistry from Southeast University (China) in 2012. Now she is an associate professor at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her current research interests focus on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in bioanalysis.

Libo Li obtained her Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (China) in 2015. Now she is an associate professor at School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her current research interests focus on the preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in precision agriculture.

Prof. Tianyan You, received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (China) in 1999. She has been working in School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering, Jiangsu University as a full professor since 2015. Her research interests concentrate in designing and preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in precision agriculture.

Prof. Hanping Mao, Professor of Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology (Ministry of Education of China) in Jiangsu University, and Member of Discipline Appraisal Group of State Council. His research interests mainly include: the intelligent agricultural equipment and technology, precision applying fertilizer and pesticide, detection technology of life information, and etc.

Prof. Kun Wang, Professor at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His Ph.D. was obtained from Nanjing University (China) in 2005 under the direction of Prof. Hongyuan Chen. His research interests concentrate in designing and preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in analytical chemistry, energy, and environment.
